title: Chapter 31: 847K Souls
content: Evelyn's office looks like a conspiracy board had a baby with a law review.

Every wall is covered — legal briefs tacked up with colored pins, printed screenshots from the audit arranged in chronological clusters, Leo's data visualizations pinned where the light hits them best. Red yarn connects related documents. I'm not joking. Red yarn. My mother has turned her office into a true-crime documentary set, and she's only been at this for forty-eight hours.

The smell of fresh laser-printer toner hangs in the air, sharp and chemical, layered over the staleness of office coffee that's been sitting on the warmer since this morning. I sip it anyway. It tastes like burnt pennies and regret, but I need the caffeine. My body still runs on the stuff even if my emotional engine is cold storage.

Evelyn stands at the center of it all, hands on hips, studying the wall like an architect reviewing blueprints. Which, I guess, she is. Each count is a load-bearing wall. Each piece of evidence is a beam. She's been building this case like a structure that has to hold the weight of — well.
She turns to me. Her voice shifts registers, dropping into that low, deliberate cadence she uses when she's about to say something that matters.
«Eight hundred forty-seven thousand.»
She lets the number land.

«Eight hundred forty-seven thousand souls, Chloe. That's a class action. That's not just a class action — that's THE largest class action in American legal history. Bigger than the tobacco litigation. Bigger than the opioid settlements. We need to certify the class.»
I let the number sit in my mouth like a stone. 847,000. I say it back to her, testing its weight. «Class certification means I don't just sue for myself.»

«No. You sue for all of them.» She taps the largest visualization — Leo's map of the archive, the 847,000 screens I walked through at 1 WTC, each one a person reduced to extracted data and a frozen face. «Every single one.»

The paper is warm from the printer. I touch the edge of one brief and feel its weight — the physical heft of legal argument, dense and substantial. This is what justice weighs. About four pounds of 28-pound bond paper, double-sided.

Evelyn pulls a whiteboard from behind her bookshelf. She's a woman who believes in visual aids. She uncaps a marker — blue, because she reserves red for judicial hostility and green for wins — and writes four words in clean block letters:
**NUMEROSITY · COMMONALITY · TYPICALITY · ADEQUACY**
«Rule 23,» she says. «Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. To certify a class, we need all four.»
She taps NUMEROSITY.
«Numerosity: the class must be so numerous that joining all members individually is impracticable.» She almost smiles. «Eight hundred forty-seven thousand. I think we're good.»
She taps COMMONALITY.

«Commonality: there must be questions of law or fact common to the class.» Another tap on the wall, on the Standard Infernal Contract. «They all signed the same contract. They all had the same deductions taken through the same mechanism. They all had their emotional data extracted by the same entity. The commonality is airtight.»
She taps TYPICALITY.
«Typicality: the named plaintiff's claims must be typical of the class.» She looks at me. «Your claims ARE the class's claims. Same contract, same extraction, same harm. Typical.»
She taps ADEQUACY.
Her marker hovers.

«Adequacy,» she says, and her voice shifts again — lower, cautious, the register she uses when she's circling a problem instead of solving it. «Adequacy requires that the class representative will fairly and adequately protect the interests of the class.»
The marker doesn't move.

«The class representative,» she continues, «must act in the class's best interest. Must have the same interests as the class. Must not have conflicts. And must — this is the part that matters — must be able to represent the class with conviction. With understanding. With...»
She trails off. The silence fills with the hum of her computer and the faint tick of the wall clock.
«With empathy,» I finish for her.

Evelyn sets the marker down. «Opposing counsel will argue that you're hollowed. That you can't feel what the class feels. That you can't adequately represent 847,000 people whose primary injury is emotional — emotional theft, emotional destruction, emotional void — if you yourself are incapable of experiencing the very harm you're suing over.»

She's right. I see it immediately, the way I see the load-bearing structure in Evelyn's case: a crack running through the foundation. The plaintiffs' injuries are emotional. Their representative can't feel emotion. It's a vulnerability. Maybe it's THE vulnerability.
I sit with it for a moment. I'm good at sitting with things now. That's what hollowing does. It gives you all the room in the world and nothing to put in it.
Then I say: «I don't need to feel their pain to fight for them.»
Evelyn's eyes narrow. Not in disagreement. In attention.

«I need to UNDERSTAND it,» I say. «I understand the mechanism. I understand how the contract works. I understand how the extraction protocol functions. I understand the legal remedy. Emotion didn't protect any of the 847,000. Eight hundred forty-seven thousand people FELT their emotions being stolen, and feeling it didn't stop it. Feeling it didn't save them.»
I lean forward. The screen glow from Evelyn's monitor catches my face, blue-white and cold.
«Strategy might.»

Evelyn doesn't respond right away. She picks up the marker again. Clicks it. Sets it down. Picks it up. I've never seen her fidget before. My mother doesn't fidget. She's a woman who uses her hands like instruments — precise, purposeful, no wasted motion.
Then she nods. Once.
«You're the most adequate representative I've ever had.»
She says it like she's arguing to a judge already. Clean diction, measured pace.

«Because you can't be emotionally compromised. Opposing counsel can't play on your sympathies because you don't have any. They can't intimidate you with emotional appeals because you're immune to them. They can't provoke you into a damaging admission because you have nothing provoked in you. You're depersonalized in a way that makes you legally bulletproof.»
She writes one word under ADEQUACY on the whiteboard: **STRENGTH.**
«The hollowing that destroyed your emotional life,» she says, and her voice doesn't waver but something underneath it does, something structural, «made you the perfect plaintiff.»

I don't feel the irony. I observe it. It's a clean observation, like noting the tensile strength of steel. The thing that broke me is the thing that makes me unbreakable in this specific context. If I still had my emotions, I'd appreciate the cosmic joke. Instead, I just note it.
My phone buzzes. Leo.

**[LEO]** Full dataset sent to your mom's paralegal. Hollowed Files, extraction protocols, archive locations, active Creator list — everything I pulled from the audit. It's a lot. Like, a lot a lot. Tell Evelyn the metadata folder alone is 2.3GB.

I show Evelyn the message. She's already looking at her paralegal's screen across the room — Dana, quiet and efficient, who I think might actually live in this office based on the blanket folded behind her chair. Dana's monitor is filling with file directories, cascading folders, data populating like a digital avalanche.
«Good,» Evelyn says. «Good.»
She says it twice. My mother never says anything twice. She's briefing herself in real-time, confirming that the evidence is landing where it needs to.

She sits down at her computer. Her fingers hit the keyboard with the same precision she brings to everything — fast, deliberate, no wasted keystrokes. She's drafting the class certification motion. I watch over her shoulder as the document assembles itself in real time: headings, citations, factual allegations, legal argument. She works the way I've seen architects work in documentaries — seeing the whole structure in her head and then building it section by section, each paragraph supporting the next.

I make more coffee. The pot gurgles and hisses. When I bring her a cup, she takes it without looking up, without thanking me, without breaking rhythm. That's not rudeness. That's focus. My mother at full speed is a machine with one setting.

And then — I see it. Something in the set of her shoulders. The angle of her jaw. The way she leans slightly forward over the keyboard, as if positioning herself between the screen and everything that might try to get at it. She looks like a wall. A defensive structure. Something designed to bear force and transfer it safely into the ground.
She looks like a mother.

Not soft. Never soft. But fierce. Protective in the way a seawall is protective — not because it cares about the houses behind it, but because caring and functioning are the same thing. She's building this case around me the way a wall surrounds a keep. I can't feel it as love. But I can recognize it as love, the way I can recognize a mountain even if I can't feel its height.
She's fighting for me. For all 847,000 of us. And the shape of her fight is indistinguishable from the shape of caring.
Dana's computer chimes. A notification.
Evelyn's head snaps up. Her voice shifts into a different register entirely — sharp, predatory, the sound of a lawyer who just smelled blood.
«They responded.»
Mephisto has a lawyer.

I want to sit with that fact for a moment. The Prince of Lies, the Arch-Deceiver, the entity that has been harvesting human emotional data for over a century — has legal representation. Of course he does. This is America. Even demons get due process.
The response is 200 pages.

Dana prints it. The laser printer roars to life and the toner smell thickens, that acrid chemical bite mixing with the coffee-stale air until the room smells like a law firm in purgatory. The stack of paper lands in Evelyn's hands with the solid whump of corporate aggression made physical.
She reads it in an hour.

I watch her read. Her eyes move fast, tracking, cataloging. She has a red pen in one hand and she marks the margins — not constantly, but at intervals, in bursts. Each mark is a hit. Each red slash is something she's found. Something wrong. Something weak.
When she finishes, she sets the 200 pages down on her desk with a contempt that's almost physical.
«This,» she says, «was written by someone who hasn't read a statute passed after 1950.»

She flips to her first mark. «Here. They claim the Infernal Court has exclusive jurisdiction. The Infernal Court is not a recognized tribunal under 28 U.S.C. § 1331. It has no federal jurisdiction. It has no jurisdiction, period. It's a contractual arbitration clause being dressed up as a sovereign court.»

Flip. «Here. They claim the contract is valid under the Uniform Commercial Code of 1923. The UCC wasn't even drafted until 1942, and wasn't widely adopted until the 1950s. There is no 'UCC 1923.' They made it up. Or, more likely, they're citing a version of commercial law that existed in Hell's legal department and never bothered to check if it was real.»

Flip. «Here. Emotional data extraction is a 'contractual right.' Not under current consumer protection law it isn't. The CCDPA, the Biometric Information Privacy Act, the ECPA — emotional data is treated as biometric data under at least nine state statutes. You cannot contractually waive your biometric privacy rights in New York. The provision is void as against public policy.»
Flip. Flip. Flip.

«Here. They claim you lack standing because your emotions were 'lawfully deducted.' Standing requires injury-in-fact. You had your emotional capacity removed. That IS an injury. The fact that a contract permitted it doesn't negate standing — it creates a cause of action FOR the injury.»
She spreads her hands over the marked-up pages like a surgeon presenting an x-ray.

«Fourteen instances. Fourteen times they cite authority that's been superseded, overturned, or simply doesn't exist. They cite Rowe v. Ford Motor Co. — that was reversed on appeal in 1997. They cite In re Emotional Data Commodities — that case doesn't exist. I checked Westlaw, Lexis, and three state databases. It's a phantom citation.»
She shakes her head. Not sad. Not angry. Something more dangerous: satisfied.
«He's fighting a 2026 case with a 1923 playbook.»
I look at the red slashes marching down the margins of Mephisto's response. Each one is a crack in the defendant's architecture. Each one is a beam we don't have to build because they cut it for us.
«Mephisto's lawyer,» I say. «Who is it?»
Evelyn's mouth does something that isn't quite a smile. «Firm called Bael, Veses & Associates.»
«Bael?»
«Yes. As in the demon. I'm choosing to believe it's a coincidence. I'm choosing to believe that because the alternative is that they're not even trying to hide it, and that makes me tired.»
She turns back to her screen. Cracks her knuckles. A single, sharp sound, like a joint resetting.
«Let's file.»
The e-filing system accepts the document at 4:47 PM.
Case number: 1:26-cv-04721.
Plaintiff: Chloe Hart, on behalf of herself and all others similarly situated.
Defendant: Mephisto MCN, LLC.

Seven counts:
1. Fraudulent inducement
2. Unconscionability
3. Violation of the CCDPA
4. Breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing
5. Unauthorized extraction of biometric data
6. Negligent misrepresentation
7. Conversion / kidnapping
847,000 potential class members.
The largest soul-theft case in human — or inhuman — history.

I stare at the filing on Evelyn's screen. The case caption is clean and typographically perfect: **In re: Hart v. Mephisto MCN.** My name. Against a demon. In federal court. The Southern District of New York, to be precise, in a courthouse that has heard racketeering cases and terrorism trials and antitrust actions that reshaped the American economy. Now it will hear about a deal with the devil.
I don't feel triumphant.
I don't feel anything.

But I see it. The architecture. The shape of justice built from law and data and a hundred-year-old bug in a contract that shouldn't exist. Each count is a pillar. Each piece of evidence is a stone. The whole structure rises in my mind — cathedral-precise, load-bearing, magnificent. It's the most beautiful thing I've ever seen.
And I can't feel its beauty.

I can catalog it. I can admire its proportions. I can verify that each element supports the others according to the principles of sound construction. But the ache of looking at something perfect and knowing it's perfect — that's not available to me anymore. The architecture of justice is right there, and I'm standing inside it like an empty room.
«Evelyn,» I say.

She's still at her desk, closing out the filing portal, confirming receipt. She's already moving on to the next thing — scheduling, strategy, prep for the hearing. My mother is a woman who files and then immediately starts preparing for what comes after filing. Momentum is her natural state.
«When we win,» I say, and the words come out steady, because everything comes out steady now, «when we restore the emotions — will I feel it then?»
She stops.

Her hands rest on the keyboard. The screen glow catches her face and for a second she looks her age — not old, but present. Lived-in. A woman who has spent three decades in courtrooms and still can't answer this question.

I've asked her a thousand things over the years. What's the statute of limitations on fraud? How do you impeach a hostile witness? Can you really argue in the alternative? She's always had answers. Legal minds don't tolerate uncertainty; they precedentialize it.
She turns to face me. Her voice doesn't shift into any register I recognize. It's not lawyer-voice or mother-voice or strategy-voice. It's just — her.
«I don't know.»
Two words. The most honest thing she's said since this started.

The hearing is imminent. 847,000 souls are waiting. The case is certified, the evidence is filed, the architecture stands. And the question I can't stop turning over in the empty room of my mind is simpler than any count or citation:
Is recovery possible?
The screen goes dark with inactivity. I can still see my reflection in it — pale, focused, hollow as a bell. A bell that's waiting to ring.
